ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No species-level FoodEx2 code exists for Chondrus crispus / sea moss; A00LC (Seaweeds and other algae) is the closest defensible parent category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code confirmed for raw sea moss as a food item; left null to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw sea moss is approximately 2 tablespoons (20 g), commonly consumed as a gel added to smoothies, teas, or soups.

nutrient_source.title: Sea moss, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for sea moss, raw (cr_id F14040054). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of seaweeds and algae-based foods — analogous estimate based on low-GI seaweed category data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Chondrus crispus (sea moss) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. Sea moss is a sulphated polysaccharide-rich seaweed with very low sugar (0.61 g/100g) and moderate carbohydrate (12.29 g/100g) composed largely of carrageenan, a gel-forming fibre. Based on analogous seaweed and high-fibre low-sugar plant foods in the University of Sydney GI Database, a GI of approximately 15 (low category) is a defensible conservative estimate. GL per 20 g serving = (15 × 12.29 × 0.20) / 100 = 0.37, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (67 mg/100g), modest potassium (63 mg/100g), and calcium (72 mg/100g) content
hypertension.guidance: Sea moss is low in sodium and suitable for a DASH-aligned diet; however, its potassium content is modest, so it should be paired with higher-potassium foods such as bananas or leafy greens to maximise blood-pressure benefit, and commercially prepared sea moss gels with added salt should be avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low sugar (0.61 g/100g), moderate carbohydrate (12.29 g/100g), and carrageenan polysaccharides that may attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw sea moss has a low estimated glycaemic index and very low sugar content, making it appropriate for blood-glucose management; dietary fibre is only 1.3 g/100g so it should be consumed alongside high-fibre foods, and sweetened sea moss drinks or gels with added sugar must be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.16 g/100g), zero cholesterol, low sodium, and high iron (8.9 mg/100g) supporting haematopoiesis
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sea moss is extremely low in fat and cholesterol-free, with a favourable sodium level, making it a heart-friendly food; its high iron content supports red blood cell production, though individuals on anticoagulants should consult a clinician regarding iodine and mineral interactions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High iodine content and sulphated polysaccharides with immunomodulatory properties; absence of confirmed omega-3 or strong anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sea moss contains sulphated polysaccharides with reported immunomodulatory activity and is rich in iron and calcium, but lacks confirmed omega-3 fatty acids or polyphenols; RA patients should limit intake to 1–2 tablespoons of gel per day and consult their prescriber regarding potential iodine–thyroid–medication interactions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sea moss (Chondrus crispus), also known as Irish moss or carrageen moss, is a species of red algae harvested from the rocky Atlantic coastlines of the Caribbean, Ireland, and North America. In its raw form it has a dark green-brown to purple colour, a firm gelatinous texture, and a distinctly salty, oceanic flavour. It is most commonly soaked in water and blended into a smooth gel that is added to smoothies, teas, soups, and desserts as a natural thickener and nutritional supplement. Per 100 g raw, sea moss provides approximately 49 kcal, 1.51 g protein, 0.16 g fat, 12.29 g carbohydrate (of which 0.61 g is sugar), 1.3 g dietary fibre, 67 mg sodium, 63 mg potassium, 72 mg calcium, and a notably high 8.9 mg iron. Its carbohydrate fraction is dominated by carrageenan, a sulphated polysaccharide with gel-forming, prebiotic, and potential glucose-attenuating properties. Sea moss is naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, it is low in sodium and fat, making it suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. Its very low sugar and low estimated glycaemic index (approximately 15) make it appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes dietary plans, though its fibre content is below the 3 g threshold that would independently blunt glycaemic response. High iodine content is a notable caution for individuals with thyroid conditions or those on thyroid-affecting medications. Sea moss is widely used in Caribbean and West African culinary traditions and has gained global popularity as a functional food and wellness supplement.

Is sea moss safe for thyroid conditions?
